title: NIS2 Asset Management and Configuration Baseline

compliance.assurance_rationale: Uplifted from contributing: SPL directly addresses Art.21(2)(i) requirements with specific, actionable monitoring.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that NIS2 Art.21(2)(i) (Human resources and access control) is enforced — Splunk UC-22.2.13: NIS2 Asset Management and Configuration Baseline.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.2.13' running on index vulnerability and supporting catalogue-defined data sources, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

dataSources: ES Asset Framework (`asset_lookup_by_str`, `asset_lookup_by_cidr`), CIM Network_Traffic data model, `index=vulnerability` (asset discovery scans)
app: Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263), Splunk Add-on for Qualys (Splunkbase 2964)
spl:
| tstats `summariesonly` dc(All_Traffic.dest_port) as ports, sum(All_Traffic.bytes) as total_bytes
  from datamodel=Network_Traffic.All_Traffic
  by All_Traffic.src
| rename All_Traffic.src as src
| lookup asset_lookup_by_str key AS src OUTPUT category, priority, owner, nt_host
| where isnull(category) OR category="unknown"
| sort - total_bytes
| head 100
| table src, nt_host, category, owner, ports, total_bytes
description: Article 21(2)(i) includes asset management alongside access control and HR security. This use case validates that the ES asset framework is populated and current for NIS2-scoped systems, detects unknown or unmanaged assets communicating on critical networks, and tracks configuration baseline drift — ensuring the asset inventory that underpins all other NIS2 controls is reliable.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for NIS2 compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes vulnerability. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Populate ES asset framework from CMDB, vulnerability scanners, and network discovery tools; (2) schedule this search daily to find active IPs not in the asset inventory; (3) classify discovered assets by NIS2 criticality (essential service vs supporting); (4) alert on high-traffic unknown assets; (5) track asset inventory completeness as a NIS2 KPI in UC-22.2.9 effectiveness dashboard. Indexes required: `index=vulnerability`; Lookups: `asset_lookup_by_str`.
visualization: Table (unknown active assets), Single value (% assets classified), Bar chart (assets by category), Map (asset locations if geo data available).
cimSpl: | tstats summariesonly=t count from datamodel=Network_Traffic.All_Traffic by All_Traffic.src, All_Traffic.dest, All_Traffic.dest_port | sort - count
knownFalsePositives: Operational noise during large maintenance windows, controlled exercises, or scope changes to critical entity lists.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263), Splunk Add-on for Qualys (Splunkbase 2964).
• Ensure the following data sources are available: ES Asset Framework (`asset_lookup_by_str`, `asset_lookup_by_cidr`), CIM Network_Traffic data model, `index=vulnerability` (asset discovery scans).
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Populate ES asset framework from CMDB, vulnerability scanners, and network discovery tools; (2) schedule this search daily to find active IPs not in the asset inventory; (3) classify discovered assets by NIS2 criticality (essential service vs supporting); (4) alert on high-traffic unknown assets; (5) track asset inventory completeness as a NIS2 KPI in UC-22.2.9 effectiveness dashboard.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
| tstats `summariesonly` dc(All_Traffic.dest_port) as ports, sum(All_Traffic.bytes) as total_bytes
  from datamodel=Network_Traffic.All_Traffic
  by All_Traffic.src
| rename All_Traffic.src as src
| lookup asset_lookup_by_str key AS src OUTPUT category, priority, owner, nt_host
| where isnull(category) OR category="unknown"
| sort - total_bytes
| head 100
| table src, nt_host, category, owner, ports, total_bytes
```

Understanding this SPL

**NIS2 Asset Management and Configuration Baseline (Art. 21(2)(i))** — Article 21(2)(i) includes asset management alongside access control and HR security. This use case validates that the ES asset framework is populated and current for NIS2-scoped systems, detects unknown or unmanaged assets communicating on critical networks, and tracks configuration baseline drift — ensuring the asset inventory that underpins all other NIS2 controls is reliable.

Documented **Data sources**: ES Asset Framework (`asset_lookup_by_str`, `asset_lookup_by_cidr`), CIM Network_Traffic data model, `index=vulnerability` (asset discovery scans). **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Splunk Enterprise Security (Splunkbase 263), Splunk Add-on for Qualys (Splunkbase 2964). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Uses `tstats` against accelerated summaries for data model `Network_Traffic.All_Traffic` — enable acceleration for that model.
• Renames fields with `rename` for clarity or joins.
• Enriches events using `lookup` (lookup definition + optional OUTPUT fields).
• Filters the current rows with `where isnull(category) OR category="unknown"` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.
• Limits the number of rows with `head`.

See also the **CIM SPL** field in this use case; enable the matching data model acceleration if you run the tstats variant in production.

Output fields: category, nt_host, owner, ports, src, total_bytes.

Step 3 — Validate
Compare with audit evidence in the source system (DLP, IDP, or application) and, if this use case underpins a control test, the ServiceNow GRC console or the evidence pack for a known time window.


Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table (unknown active assets), Single value (% assets classified), Bar chart (assets by category), Map (asset locations if geo data available).
grandmaExplanation: We stay on top of asset management and configuration baseline — so we can show we run security operations and reporting the way the cyber rules that apply to us require.
